topic: How Supply and Demand Control Everything
title: Why prices move when buyers and sellers shift

lesson.learn:
Supply and demand are the basic forces that shape prices in market economies. When demand rises faster than supply, prices tend to climb. When supply grows faster than demand, prices tend to fall. This simple interaction helps explain everything from food prices to rent, labor, oil, and electronics.

The reason the idea matters so much is that it is not just about money. Supply and demand also shape shortages, surpluses, queues, wages, and the choices companies make about what to produce.
lesson.simpler: When lots of people want something and there is not much of it, the price goes up. When there is too much and not enough buyers, the price goes down.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Supply and demand control prices because markets balance what people want against what producers can provide.
lesson.deeperDive:
In real life, supply and demand are affected by many things: income, tastes, technology, transport, taxes, weather, and expectations. A bad harvest can cut supply. A viral trend can raise demand. A tax can shift the curve. A new factory can lower costs and increase supply.

That is why economists use the model everywhere. It is simple enough to teach, but flexible enough to explain a huge range of everyday economic behavior.
lesson.funFact: Even a tiny supply shortage can push prices up fast.

people: Economists - model equilibrium and price changes
places: Factories and farms - where supply begins
Stores and apps - where prices are visible
years: 1800s - supply and demand become core economics
2020s - price shocks keep the model relevant
concepts: Equilibrium - where supply and demand meet
Elasticity - how strongly buyers or sellers respond
